ideas.Defense of common sense:
G. E. Moore's "**defense of common sense**" is one of his most characteristic and influential philosophical stances — his insistence that the ordinary, everyday beliefs of common sense (that there are material objects, that the earth has existed for many years, that other people exist and have experiences) are *more certain* than any philosophical argument that purports to cast doubt on them. This robust, anti-skeptical commonsense realism was a hallmark of Moore's philosophy and a founding impulse of analytic philosophy.

Moore was reacting especially against the **idealism** dominant in British philosophy in his youth (the absolute idealism of figures like Bradley and McTaggart), which held, on abstruse metaphysical grounds, that material things or time or the independent external world are ultimately unreal or mere appearance. Moore's response was to appeal to what we all, in fact, know perfectly well. He famously insisted that he knew, with certainty, a vast number of commonsense propositions to be true — that he had a body, that the earth existed long before his birth, that there were other human beings.

His signature strategy was to turn skeptical and idealist arguments **on their head**. When a philosopher offers an ingenious argument concluding that (say) external objects do not exist, Moore replied that the *premises* of such an argument are always *less certain* than the commonsense proposition it denies. Since I am *more* sure that "here is a hand" than I am of any abstract philosophical premise used to prove that I cannot know it, the rational response is to reject the philosophical premise, not the plain fact. As he put it, any argument against common sense is more likely to contain a false premise than to be sound. This defense of the certainty and priority of ordinary knowledge profoundly shaped twentieth-century epistemology and the ordinary-language tradition.
ideas.The naturalistic fallacy:
The "**naturalistic fallacy**" is G. E. Moore's most famous contribution to **ethics**, introduced in his landmark *Principia Ethica* (1903) — the alleged error of attempting to **define** the ethical concept **good** in terms of any **natural** (or metaphysical) property, such as pleasure, desire, evolutionary fitness, or "what we tend to approve." Moore argued that all such definitions commit a fundamental mistake, and his critique reshaped twentieth-century moral philosophy.

Moore's central claim is that "**good**" names a **simple, unanalyzable, non-natural property** — simple in the way "yellow" is simple, incapable of being broken down into or defined by anything more basic. Just as you cannot *define* the color yellow to someone who has never seen it (you can only point to instances), so "good," Moore held, is a basic quality that we grasp by a kind of intuition but cannot analyze into other terms. To *identify* goodness with any natural property — to say, as the utilitarian does, that "good" simply *means* "pleasant," or "good" *means* "what we desire to desire" — is to commit the naturalistic fallacy: it confuses the property of goodness itself with the (natural) things that merely *possess* or accompany it.

Moore's chief argument for this is the "**open question argument**." For any proposed naturalistic definition — say, "good = pleasant" — we can always sensibly ask, "This is pleasant, but is it *good*?" The question remains **open** and meaningful, not trivial or self-answering; whereas if "good" really *meant* "pleasant," the question "Is the pleasant good?" would be as empty as "Is the pleasant pleasant?" Since the question always stays genuinely open, "good" cannot be identical in meaning to any natural property. This argument, defending the autonomy and irreducibility of the ethical, was enormously influential — spurring the development of intuitionism, non-cognitivism, and metaethics as a distinct field.
ideas.Direct realism:
**Direct realism** (and, more broadly, Moore's realism about perception and the external world) is an important strand of G. E. Moore's philosophy — his defense of the commonsense view that in perception we are directly aware of, and gain knowledge of, a **mind-independent external world**, against the idealist and skeptical theories that would deny or complicate this. Moore's realism was part of his broader revolt, with Bertrand Russell, against the reigning British idealism at the turn of the twentieth century.

Moore's realism has two dimensions. Metaphysically, he insisted (famously in his early paper "The Refutation of Idealism," 1903) on the **independent existence** of the objects of perception — that the things we perceive, unlike our sensations of them, exist independently of any mind perceiving them. He attacked the idealist principle *esse est percipi* ("to be is to be perceived"), arguing that idealists illegitimately conflate the *act* of sensing (which is mental) with the *object* sensed (which need not be), and that when this confusion is exposed, there is no good reason to deny that perceived objects exist mind-independently.

Epistemologically, Moore defended our **direct knowledge** of external things — that we perceive tables, hands, and trees themselves, and know them to exist, rather than being trapped behind a veil of private ideas or sense-data through which the external world must be dubiously inferred. (Moore in fact wrestled at length and inconclusively with the status of "sense-data" and the exact relation between them and physical objects, and his position here was more tentative than his robust common-sense pronouncements suggest.) But the guiding thrust was firmly realist: the external world is real, independent of us, and directly accessible to perception and knowledge. This realism, and Moore's method of taking ordinary knowledge as the fixed point against which theories must be measured, deeply shaped analytic epistemology.
ideas.Here is one hand:
"**Here is one hand**" is the centerpiece of G. E. Moore's celebrated "**Proof of an External World**" (1939) — one of the most famous, discussed, and deceptively simple arguments in modern philosophy. Confronting the long-standing skeptical challenge (given classic form by Kant, who thought it a "scandal" that philosophy had produced no proof of the existence of things outside us), Moore offered a startlingly direct response.

In a lecture, Moore held up his hands and performed his "proof." Raising his right hand and making a gesture, he said "**Here is one hand**"; raising his left, "**and here is another**." From these two premises he concluded that at least two external objects (his hands) exist, and therefore that an external world exists. Moore insisted this was a perfectly **rigorous proof**: its premises were known to be true, they were different from the conclusion, and the conclusion genuinely followed. He knew he had two hands with far greater certainty than he could know any philosophical premise to the contrary.

The proof looks almost comically simple — even question-begging — and it has provoked enormous debate about what Moore was really doing. His deeper point was **epistemological and strategic**: he was not so much refuting the skeptic's arguments in detail as challenging their force. Since we are *more certain* of ordinary commonsense truths (like "here is a hand") than we could ever be of the abstract philosophical premises used to deny them, it is always more reasonable to reject the skeptical argument than to give up the plain fact. The skeptic's premises are the weaker link. Moore thus reversed the usual direction of argument — using common-sense certainties to judge philosophical theories, rather than the reverse. Wittgenstein's late notebook *On Certainty* was a sustained meditation provoked by Moore's proof.

works.Principia Ethica:
*Principia Ethica* (1903) is G. E. Moore's masterpiece and one of the most influential works of **ethics** of the twentieth century — a book that reshaped moral philosophy and helped inaugurate the analytic approach to the subject, shifting attention from substantive moral doctrines to the **analysis of ethical concepts** (metaethics). It also had a profound cultural influence, becoming something of a bible for the Bloomsbury Group.

The book's most famous doctrine is Moore's argument that **good** is a **simple, unanalyzable, non-natural property**. Moore contends that "good" cannot be *defined* in terms of anything else — it is a basic quality, like the color yellow, that we can recognize but not break down into constituent parts. Any attempt to define good in terms of some **natural** property (pleasure, desire, evolutionary survival) or metaphysical property commits what he names the "**naturalistic fallacy**." His chief weapon is the "**open question argument**": for any proposed definition ("good = pleasant"), it remains a genuinely *open*, meaningful question whether the pleasant really is good — which it would not be if the two were identical in meaning. Goodness is thus *sui generis*, known by intuition.

From this metaethical foundation Moore draws normative conclusions. Since good is indefinable, we cannot *prove* what things are good; we grasp this by direct insight. Moore was an ethical **consequentialist** (right action is that which produces the most good) and, in his famous final chapter, an advocate of "**ideal**" values: he argued that by far the most valuable things we know are "the pleasures of human intercourse [personal affection] and the enjoyment of beautiful objects" — a conclusion that deeply influenced Bloomsbury. Rigorous, austere, and endlessly debated, *Principia Ethica* launched twentieth-century metaethics.
works.A Defence of Common Sense:
"**A Defence of Common Sense**" (1925) is one of G. E. Moore's most important and characteristic essays — a mature statement of his robust commonsense realism and his method of taking ordinary knowledge as the bedrock against which philosophical theories must be tested. Moore himself regarded it as among his best work, and Wittgenstein was so provoked and fascinated by it (and by its companion "Proof of an External World") that it inspired his own late reflections in *On Certainty*.

In the essay, Moore does something deceptively simple: he lays out a long list of propositions which, he declares, he **knows with certainty to be true** — what he calls truisms of "the Common Sense view of the world." These include that he has a living human body, that the earth had existed for many years before he was born, that there are and have been many other human beings with bodies and experiences, that he has had various experiences, and so on. He insists that he *knows* all these things to be true, and that everyone else knows the corresponding facts about themselves.

Moore's philosophical point is directed against **skeptics** and **idealists** who deny or cast doubt on such commonsense certainties on the basis of abstract philosophical arguments. Moore argues that we are *more certain* of these plain truisms than we could ever be of the premises of any philosophical argument designed to overthrow them — so that when philosophy conflicts with common sense, it is philosophy, not common sense, that should give way. He also insists on a sharp distinction between *knowing* a proposition to be true (which we plainly do) and being able to give a correct *analysis* of it (which may be difficult and disputed). This defense of the certainty and priority of common knowledge is a landmark of analytic epistemology.
works.Proof of an External World:
"**Proof of an External World**" (1939) is one of G. E. Moore's most famous and provocative essays — his direct and startlingly simple response to the philosophical **skepticism** about the existence of the external world, and to Kant's remark that it remains a "scandal to philosophy" that no satisfactory *proof* of "the existence of things outside of us" had ever been given. Moore set out to supply exactly such a proof.

The essay is remembered above all for its dramatic and deceptively plain demonstration. Moore raises his hands in turn and declares: "**Here is one hand**," and "**here is another**." From these two premises — that here is one hand, and here is another hand — he concludes that at least two external objects exist, and therefore that there exists an external world. Moore contends, quite seriously, that this constitutes a **perfectly rigorous proof**: the premises are known to be true, they are genuinely distinct from the conclusion, and the conclusion follows validly. He knew that he had hands, he insisted, far more certainly than he knew the truth of any philosophical premise that might be used to deny it.

The proof has generated enormous discussion, precisely because it seems both obviously right and philosophically unsatisfying (it appears to beg the very question the skeptic raises). But Moore's underlying strategy is subtle and characteristic: rather than refuting skeptical arguments point by point, he insists that our certainty of ordinary facts like "here is a hand" *outweighs* the certainty of any abstract premise a skeptic could offer, so that the reasonable response to a skeptical argument is to conclude that one of its premises must be false. The essay, together with "A Defence of Common Sense," powerfully shaped debates about skepticism, certainty, and knowledge, and directly inspired Wittgenstein's *On Certainty*.
